text:
Admission Date:  1975-09-04              Discharge Date:   2015-08-17

Date of Birth:  1990-12-21             Sex:   M

Service: MEDICINE

Allergies:
fish / Spiriva with HandiHaler / Lithium

Attending:[**Attending Info 8238**]
Chief Complaint:
cough and new O2 requirements

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
none

History of Present Illness:
65M h/o esophageal dysmotility, aspiration PNA s/p PEG
tube,Churg Sutton, COPD and MS presents with 1 day of SOB and
fevers. Today 7 AM at Samaritan Healthcare pt looked disheveled,
glassy eyes, flushed, temp 100.4 HR 99. RR 28. Put in bed and on
O2 at 3-6L but never really got into the 90s (around 88%). Pt
was coughing and nurse Heidi Miller wheeze on the right with
decreased sounds on the right. Gave duoneb and he coughed up
yellow thick sputum. Pt never lost consciousness. He then
complained of midsternal discomfort, then developed nausea prior
to arrival of EMTs but no vomiting. Then c/o L sided chest pain
without radiation. Of note, pt does get 25mg morphine q6hrs prn
for chronic back pain (usually receives all doses) and also uses
a fentanyl patch.
.
In addition, he was recently treated for a urinary tract
infection - OSH Ucx 1982-01-19: Proteus Guzman to cefepime. He also
reports having a chronic right ear infection that he told was
"granuloma" and has been using cipro ear drops for this.
.
On arrival to the ED, vital signs were 100.6 97 107/54 28
92%10L.
Pt was noted to be tachypneic, uncomfortable, with loud wet
cough on exam. Had bilateral diffuse rhonchi and wheezing. G
tube with mild purulent discharge.
Labs showed
8.8>37.1<241.  PMNs 81%, eos 5.5%
Trop-T: 0.02
chemistry: 139/4.1; 101/28; 17/0.5; 111
proBNP: 83
UA negative
ABG while on bipap 7.44/44/104/31/4
CXR showed bilateral lower lobe infiltrates.
Pt ws given methylpred 125mg IV, nebs, vanc/cefepime/flagyl.
Received 2mg IV morphine for chest pain whch subsequently
improved. He was started on Bipap for respiratory distress which
was discontinued after 30 minutes.  Pt was admitted to the ICU
for presumed COPD exacerbation vs pneumonia. Vitals prior to
transfer: 98.5 ??????F (36.9 ??????C), Pulse: 85, RR: 20, BP: 97/47,
O2Sat: 93, O2Flow: NRB.
.
In the Cynthia Rice, pt satting low 90s on NRB and 6L NC. Within the
first 20 minutes of his arrival pt was actively becoming
increasingly somnolent. He had to be shaken to arouse and answer
questions but was arousable. BP 110s/70s HR 80s. Repeat ABG
showed 7.42/44/68/30/3. Lactate:0.7. After roughly 1 hour of
sleeping pt woke up and was fully alert, oriented and
interactive. Reported feeling tired after not sleeping all
night.
.
Review of systems:
(+) Per HPI
(-) Denies fever, chills, night sweats, recent weight loss or
gain. Denies headache, sinus tenderness, rhinorrhea or
congestion. Denies cough, or wheezing. Denies palpitations, or
weakness. Denies nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation,
abdominal pain, or changes in bowel habits. Denies dysuria,
frequency, or urgency. Denies arthralgias or myalgias. Denies
rashes or skin changes.


Past Medical History:
h/o PE s/p IVC filter
MS
chronic back pain
s/p spinal fusion
depression
bipolar disorder
hypothyroidism
henia repair
multiple spinal compression fractures (thought to be secondary
to prednisone use)
COPD with 2L NC at home
OSA with CPAP at home


Social History:
75 pack year h/o smoking; quit several years ago. H/o heavy
drinking quit several years ago.


Family History:
FAMILY HISTORY:
non-contributory


Physical Exam:
ON ADMISSION:
Vitals: T: 98.9 BP:108/54 P:87 R:15 O2:  91% 100% facemask and
6L NC.
General: no acute distress. somnolent but arousable.
HEENT: Sclera anicteric, MMM, oropharynx clear
Neck: supple, JVP not elevated, no LAD
Lungs: very poor air movemment throughout but clear to
auscultation bilaterally.
CV: Regular rate and rhythm, normal S1 + S2, no murmurs, rubs,
gallops. distant heart sounds
Abdomen: NTND, hypoactive bowel sounds, Gtube site without
drainage or erythema.
GU: no foley
Ext: warm, well perfused, 2+ pulses, no clubbing, cyanosis or
edema.
Pt with asterixis.

ON DISCHARGE 2015-08-17:
Vitals: T 98.2, P 71 (50-70s), BP 116/69 (90-130/60-70s), R 18,
O2 95% on 4L NC.
Gen: alert, well appearing, NAD
Psych: normal affect/mood
CV: RRR no m/g
Chest: fentanyl patch noted on anterior R chest
Lung: slight bibasilar crackles, no wheeze, good air movement.
Occasional "junky" cough but no rhonchi on exam.
Abdomen: PEG tube noted, +BS, nondistended, mild R abd
tenderness (which pt says is chronic since PEG placement)
Extremities: no LE edema


Pertinent Results:
CXR 1975-09-04:
IMPRESSION: Bibasilar opacities likely represent combination of
atelectasis and scarring given patient's history of chronic
aspiration. However, underlying pneumonia in the lower lobes
cannot be ruled out.
.
2016-06-30 RUQ U/S
IMPRESSION:
1. Minimally heterogeneous liver. Echogenic portal triads.
2. Soft tissue lesion in the gallbladder fundus with
calcification.
Malignancy should be considered. This should be further
evaluated by MRCP.

MRCP 1975-08-12:
Findings consistent with gallbladder adenomyomatosis
(hyperplastic
cholecystosis).

1975-08-12 06:20AM BLOOD WBC-5.4 RBC-3.35* Hgb-10.5* Hct-33.1*
MCV-99* MCH-31.3 MCHC-31.6 RDW-16.5* Plt Ct-235
1975-08-12 06:20AM BLOOD Glucose-113* UreaN-23* Creat-0.5 Na-140
K-3.7 Cl-107 HCO3-25 AnGap-12
2016-06-30 04:32AM BLOOD CK-MB-2 cTropnT-<0.01
1975-08-12 06:20AM BLOOD Calcium-8.9 Phos-2.4*# Mg-2.2

Brief Hospital Course:
# hypoxia/respiratory failure secondary to pneumonia with sepsis
- Pt with severe COPD on 2L O2 at baseline at home, now with new
O2 requirement necessitating BIPAP for 30 minutes in the ED.
Remained hemodynamically stable. H/o recurrent aspiration pna
for which a Gtube was placed previously. Current presentation of
fever, productive cough, hypoxia --> consistent w/ aspiration
PNA. Given COPD and smoking history COPD flare was also
considered. Pt was initially started on vanc/cefepime/flagyl for
pna but transitioned to levoquin/flagyl as he did not have
elevated white count and was afebrile during hospital admission.
Also, he was able to maintain stable respiratory status and
improved after 1 night of monitoring in the ICU. PE was
considered given history of PE s/p IVC filter however his Bentley
score was quite low and it was not felt that this was high on
the differential. Steroids (prednisone 60mg) and nebs were
started.
At time of discharge he is feeling well with an ongoing mild
productive cough but no dyspnea or wheezing. He will continue PO
levofloxacin + metronidazole through 1988-03-07 (day 1 of
antibiotics was 1975-09-04).  He will also continue a steroid taper
regimen of: prednisone 40mg x 3 days starting 2015-03-26, then 20mg
x 2 days starting 02-26, then 10mg x 2 days starting 01-24, then
off.
He has not weaned off oxygen yet and remains on 4L NC. We
anticipate that with steroid tapering and chest PT, he will wean
back to his baseline.
.
#chest pain - pt c/o sharp left sided chest pain resolved with
morphine in ED. Serial ECGs showed no evidence of changes and
serial cardiac enzymes negative x3. He had missed several doses
of home morphine for his chronic back pain while in the ED and
being transported and it was felt that he got behind on his pain
control. Possibly a pleuritic component of pain from pneumonia
but this was less likely. Chest pain resolved after pt was
re-initiated on home regimen of SL morphine with fentanyl patch.
.
# AMS - On arrival to the ICU was sleepy but arousable. ABG was
normal. After sleeping for roughly 30 minutes he was fully awake
and alert. He stated he was just tired from being up all night
not feeling well the night before. Electrolytes were within
normal limits. Some component of lethargy also attributed to the
2mg IV morphine he received for chest pain in the ED. Pt did
have asterixis on exam however and there was concern for
cirrhosis/hepatic encephalopathy, although coags and LFTs were
normal. RUQ u/s was ordered, see re gallblader mass below. Pt
also has history of depression, MS, and h/o ECT; all of which
could have predisposed him to any mental status changes by other
inciting factors.
.
# gallbladder mass - RUQ US done to eval for cirrhosis; showed
normal liver but a mass in the gallbladder.  MRCP done on 12-01
was reassuring as it showed gallbladder adenomyomatosis (aka
hyperplastic cholecystosis) without concern for malignancy.
.
#dysuria - pt c/o several days of dysuria which felt to him like
his last UTI. UA neg and culture showed only bacterial growth
suggestive of skin/fecal contamination.

# Eosinophilic pneumonia vs Churg-Sutton: He is on
azathioprine, and his last visit with a pulmonologist at Lifecare Hospitals Of North Carolina
was in 1973-09-19.  He should have f/u with Dr. Kim
(pulmonology).

# Back pain: continued home fentanyl patch
.
# Hypothyroidism: Continued levothyroxine
.
# OSA: Continue home CPAP.
.
# Esophageal dysmotility: Continue Reglan

# HLD: continued pravastatin
.
# Depression: continue celexa 30 mg qday
.
# Bipolar d/o: continue quetiapine 12.5 mg hs prn, trazodone 50
mg
hs prn
.
# Osteopenia: continue home Ca + Vit D

# DISPO: back to Los Robles Hospital & Medical Center - East Campus.

TRANSITIONAL ISSUES:
- continue antibiotics as above
- continue prednisone taper as above
- wean oxygen
- follow up with pulmonary Dr. Kim
- blood cultures are pending at time of discharge

Medications on Admission:
Pravastatin 40 qhs
Lansoprazole 30 daily
Citalopram 30 daily
Quetiapine 100 qhs
Trazodone 25 qhs
Gabapentin 800 tid
Metoclopramide 5 QID
Fentanyl 50mcg patch q72h
Bactrim DS 1 tab qod
Azathioprine 150 daily
Levothyroxine 25 mcg daily
Calcium + vit D tid
Duonebs QID

Discharge Medications:
1. albuterol sulfate 2.5 mg /3 mL (0.083 %) Solution for
Nebulization Kline: One (1)  Inhalation Q6H (every 6 hours) as
needed for shortness of breath or wheezing.
2. ipratropium bromide 0.02 % Solution Kline: One (1)  Inhalation
Q6H (every 6 hours) as needed for shortness of breath or
wheezing.
3. pravastatin 20 mg Tablet Kline: Two (2) Tablet PO DAILY
(Daily).
4. citalopram 20 mg Tablet Kline: 1.5 Tablets PO DAILY (Daily).
5. trazodone 50 mg Tablet Kline: 0.5 Tablet PO HS (at bedtime) as
needed for insomnia.
6. metoclopramide 5 mg/5 mL Solution Kline: One (1)  PO QIDACHS (4
times a day (before meals and at bedtime)).
7. fentanyl 50 mcg/hr Patch 72 hr Kline: One (1) Patch 72 hr
Transdermal Q72H (every 72 hours).
8. sulfamethoxazole-trimethoprim 200-40 mg/5 mL Suspension Kline:
One (1) Tablet PO QOD ().
9. azathioprine 50 mg Tablet Kline: Three (3) Tablet PO DAILY
(Daily).
10. levothyroxine 25 mcg Tablet Kline: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY
(Daily).
11. calcium carbonate 200 mg calcium (500 mg) Tablet, Chewable
Kline: One (1) Tablet, Chewable PO TID (3 times a day).
12. cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 400 unit Tablet Kline: One (1)
Tablet PO DAILY (Daily).
13. bisacodyl 10 mg Suppository Kline: One (1) Suppository Rectal
DAILY (Daily) as needed for constipation.
14. prednisone 20 mg Tablet Kline: Three (3) Tablet PO DAILY
(Daily) for 7 days: 40mg x 3 days starting 2015-03-26, then 20mg x 2
days starting 02-26, then 10mg x 2 days starting 01-24, then off.

15. levofloxacin 750 mg Tablet [**Month/Year (2) **]: One (1) Tablet PO once a day
for 4 days: final day 1988-03-07.
16. metronidazole 500 mg Tablet [**Month/Day/Year **]: One (1) Tablet PO Q8H
(every 8 hours) for 4 days: final date 1988-03-07.
17. lansoprazole 30 mg Tablet,Rapid Dissolve, DR Kline: One (1)
Tablet,Rapid Dissolve, DR Kline DAILY (Daily).
18. morphine concentrate 100 mg/5 mL (20 mg/mL) Solution Kline:
1.25 mL PO Q4 () as needed for pain.
19. Seroquel 100 mg Tablet Kline: One (1) Tablet PO at bedtime.
20. gabapentin 800 mg Tablet Kline: One (1) Tablet PO three times
a day.


Discharge Disposition:
Extended Care

Facility:
Los Robles Hospital & Medical Center - East Campus - 7298 Daniel Ridges
Hannabury, ND 05355

Discharge Diagnosis:
Primary: Aspiration/Bacterial Pneumonia with COPD exacerbation
Secondary:
Back Pain
Gallbladder mass
Multiple sclerosis
Dysphagia with chronic G-tube


Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - requires assistance or aid (walker
or cane).


Discharge Instructions:
You were hospitalized for aspiration pneumonia/pneumonitis.
This is being treated with antibiotics as well as steroids as
you also have COPD.  An ultrasound showed an abnormal area of
your gallbladder; a follow-up MRI showed gallbladder
thickening/masses which are likely benign and require no further
evaluation.
You should follow up with pulmonary Dr. Kim.

TRANSITIONAL ISSUES
- continue antibiotics
- continue prednisone taper
- wean oxygen
- follow up with pulmonary Dr. Kim
- blood cultures are pending at time of discharge


Followup Instructions:
